APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses relation extraction and event-centered modeling using neural methods (event-based recursive neural networks with pre-trained word2vec embeddings and a precision-biased loss) which squarely falls within top NLP/ML venues. It includes expected components for the community: annotated data, comparisons to rule-based baselines (TABARI), standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy), and a multi-document aggregation pipeline for temporal analysis. Minor scope concerns arise from the heavy application orientation (geopolitical trend visualization), reliance on news titles rather than full text, and some subjective evaluation of trend relevance, but overall the topic, methods, and audience alignment make it a good fit. | B(4): The paper sits squarely in NLP relation/event extraction and temporal aggregation, topics well within top-tier NLP/ML venues (it proposes event-centered recursive neural models, a precision-biased loss, and a multi-document aggregation pipeline). It evaluates on a large news-title corpus (3.3M titles) with a labelled relation dataset, compares against rule-based baselines (TABARI), and reports standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy), which matches community expectations. The main scope concern is its narrow use of news titles (acknowledged limitation) and the strongly application-specific geopolitical focus, which may reduce broad methodological impact but does not remove relevance to the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses relation extraction and event-centered modeling using neural methods (event-based recursive neural networks with pre-trained word2vec embeddings and a precision-biased loss) which squarely falls within top NLP/ML venues. It includes expected components for the community: annotated data, comparisons to rule-based baselines (TABARI), standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy), and a multi-document aggregation pipeline for temporal analysis. Minor scope concerns arise from the heavy application orientation (geopolitical trend visualization), reliance on news titles rather than full text, and some subjective evaluation of trend relevance, but overall the topic, methods, and audience alignment make it a good fit.

It evaluates on a large news-title corpus (3.3M titles) with a labelled relation dataset, compares against rule-based baselines (TABARI), and reports standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy), which matches community expectations. The main scope concern is its narrow use of news titles (acknowledged limitation) and the strongly application-specific geopolitical focus, which may reduce broad methodological impact but does not remove relevance to the venue.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and the main contributions are stated early (event-based recursive models, a precision-oriented loss, and a multi-document aggregation pipeline). Notation is consistent and equations are given (e.g. w(d)=log((1+P(d))/(1+N(d))) ), hyperparameters are reported, and the writing quality is described as good. Figures/tables and visualizations are implied and likely informative, but some presentation limitations remain (relying only on titles, the dominant NEU class, and subjective trend evaluation), which slightly reduce clarity in the evaluation and results sections. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem statement through method and experiments, and the main contributions (event-based recursive model, precision-oriented loss, and aggregation pipeline) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and concrete elements like the precision penalty α and the aggregation formula w(d)=log((1+P(d))/(1+N(d))) are defined, and datasets/ hyperparameters are reported. Figures, examples, and visualizations (time-series smoothing) are presented and informative, and the writing quality is described as good; minor presentation caveats (e.g., explicit title-only limitations and a subjective evaluation of trend relevance) are acknowledged but do not impede clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized and the main contributions are stated early (event-based recursive models, a precision-oriented loss, and a multi-document aggregation pipeline). Notation is consistent and equations are given (e.g. w(d)=log((1+P(d))/(1+N(d))) ), hyperparameters are reported, and the writing quality is described as good. Figures/tables and visualizations are implied and likely informative, but some presentation limitations remain (relying only on titles, the dominant NEU class, and subjective trend evaluation), which slightly reduce clarity in the evaluation and results sections.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem statement through method and experiments, and the main contributions (event-based recursive model, precision-oriented loss, and aggregation pipeline) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and concrete elements like the precision penalty α and the aggregation formula w(d)=log((1+P(d))/(1+N(d))) are defined, and datasets/ hyperparameters are reported. Figures, examples, and visualizations (time-series smoothing) are presented and informative, and the writing quality is described as good; minor presentation caveats (e.g., explicit title-only limitations and a subjective evaluation of trend relevance) are acknowledged but do not impede clarity.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an important practical problem—extracting and aggregating international alliance/opposition signals from noisy news—with a coherent pipeline combining event-centered recursive neural models, a precision-biased loss, and a multi-document aggregation/visualization method. These components are practically useful (trained on a large news-title corpus and evaluated against a rule-based baseline like TABARI) and could influence applied geopolitics and relation-extraction work. However, reliance on titles, a relatively small annotated set, and subjective trend evaluation limit generality and make the contribution more of a useful applied advance than a field-changing result. | B(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem—automated extraction and multi-document aggregation of interstate alliance/opposition from news—which matters for geopolitical monitoring. Its combination of event-centered recursive neural models, a precision-biased loss tailored for downstream aggregation, and a scalable aggregation/smoothing pipeline (trained on 3.3M titles and an annotated set of ~5k pairs) is a useful, application-oriented contribution. However, reliance on titles only, a modest annotated dataset, and subjective evaluation of trend relevance limit generality and make the contribution more incremental than transformative. Overall it is likely to be useful to practitioners and to open follow-up work, but not to radically change the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—extracting and aggregating international alliance/opposition signals from noisy news—with a coherent pipeline combining event-centered recursive neural models, a precision-biased loss, and a multi-document aggregation/visualization method. However, reliance on titles, a relatively small annotated set, and subjective trend evaluation limit generality and make the contribution more of a useful applied advance than a field-changing result.

Its combination of event-centered recursive neural models, a precision-biased loss tailored for downstream aggregation, and a scalable aggregation/smoothing pipeline (trained on 3.3M titles and an annotated set of ~5k pairs) is a useful, application-oriented contribution. However, reliance on titles only, a modest annotated dataset, and subjective evaluation of trend relevance limit generality and make the contribution more incremental than transformative. Overall it is likely to be useful to practitioners and to open follow-up work, but not to radically change the field.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs substantial experiments: training event-centered recursive models on a large 3.3M-title corpus, an annotated set of 5,172 entity-pairs (test set of 453), compares to a rule-based baseline (TABARI), and reports precision/recall/accuracy while describing a multi-document aggregation pipeline. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and several limitations are explicitly discussed (title-only input issues, NEU class dominance, precision/recall tradeoff, subjective trend evaluation). However, the summary does not explicitly report thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses of model components (beyond mentioning an EC variant and extra lexical features) and code/dataset release is not mentioned, limiting full reproducibility—hence a solid but not perfect rating. | B(3): The paper presents substantive experiments (training event-centered recursive models on a 3.3M-title corpus, an annotated set of 5,172 entity-pairs and a 453-relation test set) and compares to a rule-based baseline (TABARI), and it explicitly evaluates a precision-biased loss and a multi-document aggregation pipeline. However, the annotated/test sets are relatively small, trend-evaluation is admitted to be subjective/limited, and the summary does not report systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses of key choices (e.g., loss weight α, aggregation/window parameters) or broad robustness checks. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations (titles-only, NEU dominance, precision/recall tradeoffs) are openly discussed, and hyperparameters are reported, but code/data availability is not mentioned which constrains full reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs substantial experiments: training event-centered recursive models on a large 3.3M-title corpus, an annotated set of 5,172 entity-pairs (test set of 453), compares to a rule-based baseline (TABARI), and reports precision/recall/accuracy while describing a multi-document aggregation pipeline. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and several limitations are explicitly discussed (title-only input issues, NEU class dominance, precision/recall tradeoff, subjective trend evaluation). However, the summary does not explicitly report thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses of model components (beyond mentioning an EC variant and extra lexical features) and code/dataset release is not mentioned, limiting full reproducibility—hence a solid but not perfect rating.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents substantive experiments (training event-centered recursive models on a 3.3M-title corpus, an annotated set of 5,172 entity-pairs and a 453-relation test set) and compares to a rule-based baseline (TABARI), and it explicitly evaluates a precision-biased loss and a multi-document aggregation pipeline. However, the annotated/test sets are relatively small, trend-evaluation is admitted to be subjective/limited, and the summary does not report systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses of key choices (e.g., loss weight α, aggregation/window parameters) or broad robustness checks. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations (titles-only, NEU dominance, precision/recall tradeoffs) are openly discussed, and hyperparameters are reported, but code/data availability is not mentioned which constrains full reproducibility.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper combines an event-centered recursive neural network architecture with a precision-oriented loss and a multi-document aggregation pipeline (w(d)=log((1+P)/(1+N)) plus smoothing) to extract and visualize geopolitically relevant POS/NEG relations. These elements are reasonably well-differentiated from prior rule-based event coding (TABARI/KEDS/CAMEO) and apply neural methods to an eventive relation setting, but they mostly adapt and combine existing techniques (RNNs, cost-sensitive loss, aggregation/log-ratio smoothing) rather than introduce a fundamentally new paradigm. Overall this reads as a moderate, non-trivial incremental contribution rather than a highly novel breakthrough. | B(3): The paper applies known tools (recursive neural networks, pre-trained word2vec embeddings, and multi-document aggregation) to a specific relation-extraction task (alliances/opposition) and introduces pragmatic adaptations: an event-centered RNN variant, a precision-biased cross-entropy loss that penalizes false positives, and a simple log-ratio daily aggregation with smoothing. These are useful and targeted extensions for noisy news-title data and downstream aggregation, but they do not constitute a fundamentally new paradigm in relation extraction or event modeling. Overall the work is a moderate, domain-focused extension of existing methods rather than a highly novel methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines an event-centered recursive neural network architecture with a precision-oriented loss and a multi-document aggregation pipeline (w(d)=log((1+P)/(1+N)) plus smoothing) to extract and visualize geopolitically relevant POS/NEG relations. These elements are reasonably well-differentiated from prior rule-based event coding (TABARI/KEDS/CAMEO) and apply neural methods to an eventive relation setting, but they mostly adapt and combine existing techniques (RNNs, cost-sensitive loss, aggregation/log-ratio smoothing) rather than introduce a fundamentally new paradigm. Overall this reads as a moderate, non-trivial incremental contribution rather than a highly novel breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies known tools (recursive neural networks, pre-trained word2vec embeddings, and multi-document aggregation) to a specific relation-extraction task (alliances/opposition) and introduces pragmatic adaptations: an event-centered RNN variant, a precision-biased cross-entropy loss that penalizes false positives, and a simple log-ratio daily aggregation with smoothing. These are useful and targeted extensions for noisy news-title data and downstream aggregation, but they do not constitute a fundamentally new paradigm in relation extraction or event modeling. Overall the work is a moderate, domain-focused extension of existing methods rather than a highly novel methodological breakthrough.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The core claims are generally supported: the paper trains event-centered recursive models on an annotated corpus (5,172 entity pairs / 2,166 titles) and compares against a rule-based baseline (TABARI), showing that the precision-biased cross-entropy (α) increases precision with only a small recall loss. The mathematical components used for aggregation (w(d)=log((1+P)/(1+N)) and weighted smoothing) are simple and coherent, and no formal proofs were required for these procedural choices. Methodologically there are minor weaknesses: using only titles (which the authors acknowledge) can impair parsing and generalization, the multi-document trend evaluation is described as subjective and limited, and no statistical significance testing is reported. These issues are important but do not overturn the experimental support for the paper’s main technical claims. | B(3): The paper states clear technical claims (event-centered recursive NN, a precision-biased loss with penalty factor α, and a log-ratio aggregation w(d)=log((1+P(d))/(1+N(d))) plus smoothing) and reports experiments on an annotated set (5,172 pairs / 2,166 titles) and a 453-relation test set, with comparisons to TABARI and rule-based baselines — so the approach is implemented and evaluated. The mathematical elements described are simple and correct as given, and favoring precision for downstream aggregation is a reasonable design choice. However, the summary omits crucial experimental details (no quantitative results, no mention of statistical significance tests, and limited size/coverage since only titles were used), and the aggregation/trend evaluation is admitted to be subjective, which weakens support for the broader claims and generalizability. Overall, the work is technically plausible but has gaps in reported experimental rigor and evidence supporting wider applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The core claims are generally supported: the paper trains event-centered recursive models on an annotated corpus (5,172 entity pairs / 2,166 titles) and compares against a rule-based baseline (TABARI), showing that the precision-biased cross-entropy (α) increases precision with only a small recall loss. The mathematical components used for aggregation (w(d)=log((1+P)/(1+N)) and weighted smoothing) are simple and coherent, and no formal proofs were required for these procedural choices. Methodologically there are minor weaknesses: using only titles (which the authors acknowledge) can impair parsing and generalization, the multi-document trend evaluation is described as subjective and limited, and no statistical significance testing is reported. These issues are important but do not overturn the experimental support for the paper’s main technical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (event-centered recursive NN, a precision-biased loss with penalty factor α, and a log-ratio aggregation w(d)=log((1+P(d))/(1+N(d))) plus smoothing) and reports experiments on an annotated set (5,172 pairs / 2,166 titles) and a 453-relation test set, with comparisons to TABARI and rule-based baselines — so the approach is implemented and evaluated. The mathematical elements described are simple and correct as given, and favoring precision for downstream aggregation is a reasonable design choice. However, the summary omits crucial experimental details (no quantitative results, no mention of statistical significance tests, and limited size/coverage since only titles were used), and the aggregation/trend evaluation is admitted to be subjective, which weakens support for the broader claims and generalizability. Overall, the work is technically plausible but has gaps in reported experimental rigor and evidence supporting wider applicability.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper compares against relevant event-coding baselines (TABARI) and discusses rule-based systems like KEDS/CAMEO, and evaluation uses standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy) on the same news-title data. However, it does not appear to include more recent supervised or neural relation-extraction baselines for sentence-level POS/NEG classification, so key contemporary comparisons are missing. The related-work coverage is described as thorough and differences (precision-oriented loss, aggregation pipeline) are explained, but the absence of modern ML baselines and no clear statement about prior methods performing comparably reduce the completeness of the comparison. | B(4): The paper evaluates against appropriate rule-based event-coding baselines (TABARI, with KEDS/CAMEO discussed) and uses standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy), including a clear precision-focused analysis and discussion of the precision/recall tradeoff. However, the summary does not report comparisons to more recent neural relation-extraction baselines, and it is unclear whether baseline runs were performed under identical data/training/evaluation conditions or whether prior methods sometimes match or exceed performance. Related work coverage is described as thorough, so the omissions appear to be minor rather than fundamental.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against relevant event-coding baselines (TABARI) and discusses rule-based systems like KEDS/CAMEO, and evaluation uses standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy) on the same news-title data. However, it does not appear to include more recent supervised or neural relation-extraction baselines for sentence-level POS/NEG classification, so key contemporary comparisons are missing. The related-work coverage is described as thorough and differences (precision-oriented loss, aggregation pipeline) are explained, but the absence of modern ML baselines and no clear statement about prior methods performing comparably reduce the completeness of the comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against appropriate rule-based event-coding baselines (TABARI, with KEDS/CAMEO discussed) and uses standard metrics (precision/recall/accuracy), including a clear precision-focused analysis and discussion of the precision/recall tradeoff. However, the summary does not report comparisons to more recent neural relation-extraction baselines, and it is unclear whether baseline runs were performed under identical data/training/evaluation conditions or whether prior methods sometimes match or exceed performance. Related work coverage is described as thorough, so the omissions appear to be minor rather than fundamental.

main_claims: An event-based, recursive neural network approach for identifying alliance and opposition relations between countries at sentence level in English, with adaptations to the eventive nature of relations.
A precision-oriented cost function that penalizes false positives to favor precision for later multi-document aggregation.

A multi-document relation aggregation pipeline that indexes extracted relations, aggregates daily POS/NEG counts into a weighted relation score, smooths over time, and visualizes trends to produce a broader geopolitical picture from noisy text.
_summary.methodology: Train event-centered recursive neural network models (including an Event-Centered EC variant) on news titles with pre-trained word2vec embeddings, add extra lexical features, and use a precision-biased cross-entropy loss (penalizing false positives by factor α) for sentence-level POS/NEG/NEU classification; aggregate sentence-level POS/NEG counts per day into w(d)=log((1+P(d))/(1+N(d))) and apply weighted mean smoothing over a temporal window to produce time-series visualizations.
datasets: 3.3 million English news titles (≈20 web news sites, 2013–early 2016)
Annotated dataset of 5,172 entity pairs (2166 titles) with labels POS/NEG/NEU
CIA World Factbook (lists of countries, nationalities, capitals, leaders)
baselines: Rule engines / event coding systems referenced (KEDS, CAMEO)
limitations: Using only titles: titles often follow different syntactic rules than sentences and parsers not trained on titles experience higher error rates (explicit drawback).
NEU class is much more represented and considered useless for aggregation.
Favoring precision (via the cost function) trades off recall (precision gain with small recall loss reported).
Evaluating trend relevance is subjective and requires domain expertise, which limited the number of tested instances.
